frontmatter.title: The generic elements functor of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}. We shall define the generic elements functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } that, intuitively, sends an element of the base to the “generic element” of the fiber of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } at that element.

li.p: Fixing an element \Gamma \vdash A, we define qA to be the upstairs map in the following pullback square that exists by representability of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }, and can be chosen by the univalence principle for the category \mathcal {C}:

Fixing a morphism { A } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ B } in \widetilde { U } over { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ \Gamma } in \mathcal {C}, we choose { qA } \xrightarrow {{ q \bar \gamma }}{ qB } to be the upper triangle determined by the universal property of the pullback as follows:

Commutation of the outer square follows from the homomorphism property of { A } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ B } in \widetilde { U }. The assignment preserves identities and compositions, so we have defined a functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } }.

frontmatter.title: Notes on models of type theory
Type theoretical notation in representable maps

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}. We shall use the following notations inspired by dependent type theory to refer to elements of U and E:

ol.li: We shall write { \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right )} to refer to a morphism { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ A }}{ U }.

Given { \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right )}, we shall write { \left ( \Gamma \vdash a : A \right )} to refer to a morphism { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ a }}{ E } such that the composite \Gamma \xrightarrow {a}E \xrightarrow {p}U is equal to { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ A }}{ U }.

frontmatter.title: The generic elements functor of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}. We shall define the generic elements functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } that, intuitively, sends an element of the base to the “generic element” of the fiber of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } at that element.

li.p: Fixing an element \Gamma \vdash A, we define qA to be the upstairs map in the following pullback square that exists by representability of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }, and can be chosen by the univalence principle for the category \mathcal {C}:

Fixing a morphism { A } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ B } in \widetilde { U } over { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ \Gamma } in \mathcal {C}, we choose { qA } \xrightarrow {{ q \bar \gamma }}{ qB } to be the upper triangle determined by the universal property of the pullback as follows:

Commutation of the outer square follows from the homomorphism property of { A } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ B } in \widetilde { U }. The assignment preserves identities and compositions, so we have defined a functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } }.

frontmatter.title: The weakening counit of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, so that { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the corresponding generic elements functor. We may define a natural transformation { \widetilde { p } \circ q } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon }}{ 1 _{ \widetilde { U } } } that we shall call the weakening counit of the representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }.
For \Gamma \vdash A we define the component { \widetilde { p } qA } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _A }}{ A } to be the following cell over { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p^*A }}{ A }:

The reason to name \epsilon _A as we have done is that it witnesses the fact that “weakening” a given type \Gamma \vdash A into the context of its generic element gives you precisely the type of the generic element.

frontmatter.title: The substitution unit of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, so that { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the corresponding generic elements functor. We may define a natural transformation { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } that we shall call the substitution unit of the representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }.
We define { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } assigning to each \Gamma \vdash a : A the upper-hand depicted below:

The reason to refer to { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } as the substitution unit is that it witnesses the fact that every element of a given type arises from that type’s generic element by substitution.

frontmatter.title: Snake identity: duplicating the generic element

mainmatter.p: The following “snake identity” for the substitution unit and the weakening counit holds for any representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}:
As we shall see in the proof below, this identity states that duplicating the generic element gives you the generic element (up to weakening).

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Fixing \Gamma \vdash A, we must check that qA \xrightarrow { \eta _{ qA } }q \widetilde { p } qA \xrightarrow {q \epsilon _{ A } }qA is the identity in \widetilde { E }. First, we consider the component \eta _{qA} of the substitution unit at the generic element of A, which we understand to be the indicated cell below:
Next, we consider the whiskering q \epsilon _A of the component of the weakening counit at A by the generic elements functor:

We are trying to show that the composite \Gamma .A \xrightarrow { \delta } \Gamma .A. \widetilde { p } qA \xrightarrow {x} \Gamma .A is the identity. By the universal property of the pullback, it is enough to observe that qA \circ x \circ \delta = q \widetilde { p } qA \circ \delta = qA and p^*A \circ x \circ \delta = p^*A \circ p^* \widetilde { p } qA \circ \delta = p^*A both hold by virtue of the diagrams above.

mainmatter.p: The following “snake identity” for the substitution unit and the weakening counit holds for any representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}:
As we shall see, this law states that any element \Gamma \vdash a : A gives rise to a section of the display map { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p^*A }}{ \Gamma }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Fixing \Gamma \vdash a : A, we must check that \widetilde { p } a \xrightarrow { \widetilde { p } \eta _a} \widetilde { p } q \widetilde { p } a \xrightarrow { \epsilon _{ \widetilde { p } a}} \widetilde { p } a is the identity. Computing slightly further, this is the composite A \xrightarrow { \widetilde { p } \eta _a} \widetilde { p } qA \xrightarrow { \epsilon _A}A in \widetilde { U }. Unraveling the constructions of the weakening counit and the substitution unit, it suffices to observe that the left-hand composite \Gamma \xrightarrow { \Gamma .a} \Gamma .A \xrightarrow {p^*A} \Gamma depicted below is (by definition) the identity:

frontmatter.title: Representable maps in terms of adjunctions

mainmatter.p: A morphism { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C} is representable if and only if the induced functor { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } } between categories of elements has a right adjoint.

block.headline: Proof (\Rightarrow).
block.p: Suppose that { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } is a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}; we shall prove that the generic elements functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is right adjoint to { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }. We define the counit { \widetilde { p } \circ q } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon }}{ 1 _{ \widetilde { U } } } to be the weakening counit of the representable map, and we define the unit { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } }. We have checked both snake identities already.

block.headline: Proof (\Leftarrow).
block.p: Let there be a right adjoint { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } to { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } }, and fix a natural transformation { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ u }}{ U } with representable domain, i.e. an element u \in \widetilde { U }. We must check that the fiber product E \times _{U} \Gamma is representable.

Applying the right adjoint { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } }, we have an element qu \in \widetilde { E }, i.e. a morphism { \partial _0 { \left ( qu \right )} } \xrightarrow {{ qu }}{ E } whose (representable) domain we expect shall form the desired fiber product, but we must exhibit an appropriate projection { \partial _0 { \left ( qu \right )} } \xrightarrow {{ p_u }}{ \Gamma } making the following square cartesian:

We consider the component of the counit { \widetilde { p } \circ q } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon }}{ 1 _{ \widetilde { U } } } at u \in \widetilde { U }, from which we obtain a morphism { \widetilde { p } { \left ( qu \right )} } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _u }}{ u } in \widetilde { U }. Unraveling definitions, this is precisely a map making the square above commute, so we may define p_u : \equiv \epsilon _u. It suffices now to prove that the following square is cartesian:
Because pullbacks in presheaf categories are computed pointwise, it suffices to consider only squares with a representable apex, such as the one below in which \Delta is assumed representable:

To construct the universal map { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle e, \gamma \right \rangle } }}{ \partial _0qu } factoring e, \gamma through qu, \epsilon _u respectively, we note the square above corresponds precisely to a morphism { \widetilde { p } e } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ u } in \widetilde { U }, visualised as follows:
We can transpose { \widetilde { p } e } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ u } to obtain the following { e } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma ^ \sharp }}{ qu } in \widetilde { E }:

The factorisation qu \circ \gamma ^ \sharp = e holds by inspection of the diagram above in \widetilde { E }. The other factorisation \epsilon _u \circ \gamma ^ \sharp = \gamma holds by the snake identities of adjoints:

Uniqueness of { e } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma ^ \sharp }}{ qu } with this property can be seen as follows. We fix any { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ \partial _0qu } satisfying the two factorisation properties; this means that h can be viewed as a morphism in { e } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ qu } in \widetilde { E } satisfying (only) the additional property that \epsilon _u \circ h = \gamma:
To see that h = \gamma ^ \sharp, it therefore suffices to observe the following instance of the snake identity:

frontmatter.title: Snake identity: duplicating the generic element

mainmatter.p: The following “snake identity” for the substitution unit and the weakening counit holds for any representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}:
As we shall see in the proof below, this identity states that duplicating the generic element gives you the generic element (up to weakening).

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Fixing \Gamma \vdash A, we must check that qA \xrightarrow { \eta _{ qA } }q \widetilde { p } qA \xrightarrow {q \epsilon _{ A } }qA is the identity in \widetilde { E }. First, we consider the component \eta _{qA} of the substitution unit at the generic element of A, which we understand to be the indicated cell below:
Next, we consider the whiskering q \epsilon _A of the component of the weakening counit at A by the generic elements functor:

We are trying to show that the composite \Gamma .A \xrightarrow { \delta } \Gamma .A. \widetilde { p } qA \xrightarrow {x} \Gamma .A is the identity. By the universal property of the pullback, it is enough to observe that qA \circ x \circ \delta = q \widetilde { p } qA \circ \delta = qA and p^*A \circ x \circ \delta = p^*A \circ p^* \widetilde { p } qA \circ \delta = p^*A both hold by virtue of the diagrams above.

frontmatter.title: The substitution unit of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, so that { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the corresponding generic elements functor. We may define a natural transformation { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } that we shall call the substitution unit of the representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }.
We define { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } assigning to each \Gamma \vdash a : A the upper-hand depicted below:

The reason to refer to { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } as the substitution unit is that it witnesses the fact that every element of a given type arises from that type’s generic element by substitution.

frontmatter.title: The weakening counit of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, so that { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the corresponding generic elements functor. We may define a natural transformation { \widetilde { p } \circ q } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon }}{ 1 _{ \widetilde { U } } } that we shall call the weakening counit of the representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }.
For \Gamma \vdash A we define the component { \widetilde { p } qA } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _A }}{ A } to be the following cell over { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p^*A }}{ A }:

The reason to name \epsilon _A as we have done is that it witnesses the fact that “weakening” a given type \Gamma \vdash A into the context of its generic element gives you precisely the type of the generic element.

frontmatter.title: Representable maps in terms of adjunctions

mainmatter.p: A morphism { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C} is representable if and only if the induced functor { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } } between categories of elements has a right adjoint.

block.headline: Proof (\Rightarrow).
block.p: Suppose that { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } is a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}; we shall prove that the generic elements functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is right adjoint to { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }. We define the counit { \widetilde { p } \circ q } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon }}{ 1 _{ \widetilde { U } } } to be the weakening counit of the representable map, and we define the unit { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } }. We have checked both snake identities already.

block.headline: Proof (\Leftarrow).
block.p: Let there be a right adjoint { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } to { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } }, and fix a natural transformation { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ u }}{ U } with representable domain, i.e. an element u \in \widetilde { U }. We must check that the fiber product E \times _{U} \Gamma is representable.

Applying the right adjoint { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } }, we have an element qu \in \widetilde { E }, i.e. a morphism { \partial _0 { \left ( qu \right )} } \xrightarrow {{ qu }}{ E } whose (representable) domain we expect shall form the desired fiber product, but we must exhibit an appropriate projection { \partial _0 { \left ( qu \right )} } \xrightarrow {{ p_u }}{ \Gamma } making the following square cartesian:

We consider the component of the counit { \widetilde { p } \circ q } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon }}{ 1 _{ \widetilde { U } } } at u \in \widetilde { U }, from which we obtain a morphism { \widetilde { p } { \left ( qu \right )} } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _u }}{ u } in \widetilde { U }. Unraveling definitions, this is precisely a map making the square above commute, so we may define p_u : \equiv \epsilon _u. It suffices now to prove that the following square is cartesian:
Because pullbacks in presheaf categories are computed pointwise, it suffices to consider only squares with a representable apex, such as the one below in which \Delta is assumed representable:

To construct the universal map { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle e, \gamma \right \rangle } }}{ \partial _0qu } factoring e, \gamma through qu, \epsilon _u respectively, we note the square above corresponds precisely to a morphism { \widetilde { p } e } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ u } in \widetilde { U }, visualised as follows:
We can transpose { \widetilde { p } e } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ u } to obtain the following { e } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma ^ \sharp }}{ qu } in \widetilde { E }:

The factorisation qu \circ \gamma ^ \sharp = e holds by inspection of the diagram above in \widetilde { E }. The other factorisation \epsilon _u \circ \gamma ^ \sharp = \gamma holds by the snake identities of adjoints:

Uniqueness of { e } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma ^ \sharp }}{ qu } with this property can be seen as follows. We fix any { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ \partial _0qu } satisfying the two factorisation properties; this means that h can be viewed as a morphism in { e } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ qu } in \widetilde { E } satisfying (only) the additional property that \epsilon _u \circ h = \gamma:
To see that h = \gamma ^ \sharp, it therefore suffices to observe the following instance of the snake identity:
